zotero.abstractNote: The gender gap in science is a well-documented phenomenon, with women being underrepresented in many scientific fields. While much research has focused on the causes of this gap, less attention has been paid to the ways in which the language used to describe women's work may contribute to it. In this paper, we investigate whether the language used in scientific writing reflects gender biases. We introduce a new dataset of scientific abstracts and use it to train a model to identify gendered language. We then use this model to analyze a large corpus of scientific papers and find that women's work is described using different language than men's work, even when controlling for field and seniority. Finally, we propose a method for editing scientific writing to reduce gender bias.
zotero.conferenceName: International Conference on the Science of Science and Innovation (ICSSI)
zotero.proceedingsTitle: International Conference on the Science of Science and Innovation (ICSSI)
